title: Introduction
description: NOC 63211 – Estheticians, electrologists and related occupations | GTR Immigration In Canada’s flourishing wellness and beauty sector, NOC 63211 (Estheticians, electrologists and related occupations) professionals are the essential experts in personal care and aesthetic enhancement. Providing everything from advanced skin treatments to specialized electrolysis, these TEER 3 practitioners are vital to the operations of high-end spas, medical aesthetic clinics, and retail cosmetic departments. For immigration, this role offers a stable path via the Federal Skilled Trades or Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs), particularly for those with specialized certifications in laser technology or medical aesthetics.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level3Major Group63 – Occupations in sales and servicesSub-Major Group632 – Occupations in servicesMinor Group6321 – Hairstylists, barbers, estheticians and related occupationsNOC Code63211

duties: Give facial and other body treatments using specialized products and techniques.
Provide manicures and pedicures, including nail art and extensions.
Remove unwanted hair using electrolysis, waxing, or laser technology.
Advise clients on skin care and makeup techniques and recommend specialized products.
Clean and shape eyebrows and apply chemical peels or other specialized skin treatments.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
Completion of a college or other program in esthetics, electrolysis, or a related field is typically required.
Certification may be required by provincial regulatory bodies in certain jurisdictions.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate a steady increase in job openings as the "silver economy" (aging population) seeks more advanced anti-aging and skin health services.
labour_market_demand: The demand for estheticians is rated as Good to Very Good across most provinces, driven by a growing focus on self-care and medical aesthetics. Urban hubs like Toronto and Vancouver are seeing a high volume of openings in medical spas. There is a specific shortage of technicians trained in advanced skin rejuvenation and permanent hair removal.
Thriving Wellness Industry: Access to one of the most advanced medical aesthetic markets in North America.
Entrepreneurial Freedom: High rates of self-employment and the ability to build a personal brand.
Global Standards: Work with world-leading skincare technology and safety protocols.
pathways: Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP): Direct eligibility for those with 2 years of experience.
Provincial Nominee Program (PNP): In-demand streams in provinces like BC and Alberta.
CEC (Express Entry): Ideal for those with 12 months of Canadian salon/clinic experience.
